   step.

2. **Quotient preserves vanishing-higher-Čech** — from the long exact sequence of Čech cohomology

   higher Čech cohomology for `Cov`. Deps: (1), `lem:injective_cech_acyclic`.

3. **Sheaf-cohomology base case `H¹(U,F)=0`** — run the covariant Ext long exact sequence
   (`def:absolute_cohomology`, `lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib`) of `0 → F → I → Q → 0` at fixed

   `lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib`, `I` the 2nd Ext arg) this forces `H¹(U,F)=0`. Deps:
   `def:absolute_cohomology`, `lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib`,
   `lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib`, `lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib`, `lem:jshriek_corepr`,

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

2771	  cover (surjectivity of \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{H}\) is a local condition).
2772	  On the double overlaps form
2773	  \[
2774	    s_{i_0 i_1} = s_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - s_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}.
2775	  \]
2776	  Because the \(s_i\) all lift the single section \(s\), the difference
2777	  \(s_{i_0 i_1}\) maps to \(0\) in \(\mathcal{H}\), hence lies in the subsheaf
2778	  \(\mathcal{F}(U_{i_0 i_1})\); the family \((s_{i_0 i_1})\) is a {\v C}ech
2779	  \(1\)-cocycle for \(\mathcal{F}\). By \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
2780	  it is a coboundary: there are \(t_i \in \mathcal{F}(U_i)\) with
2781	  \(s_{i_0 i_1} = t_{i_1}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}} - t_{i_0}|_{U_{i_0 i_1}}\). Then the
2782	  corrected sections \(s_i - t_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) agree on overlaps, so by the
2783	  sheaf condition they glue to a global section of \(\mathcal{G}\) over \(U\) whose
2784	  image in \(\mathcal{H}\) is \(s\). Hence \(\mathcal{G}(U) \to \mathcal{H}(U)\) is
2785	  surjective.
2786	\end{proof}

2788	\section{Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object}
2789	
2790	The two vanishing lemmas below speak about the \emph{absolute} sheaf cohomology
2791	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F})\) of an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module over an open \(U\). Their
2792	dimension-shift proof needs three structural facts about \(H^p(U, -)\): a degree-zero
2793	identification with global sections, vanishing on injective modules, and a long exact
2794	sequence attached to a short exact sequence of modules. We realize \(H^p(U, -)\) as the
2795	\emph{Ext of a corepresenting object} in the single abelian category
2796	\(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\): there is an
2797	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresenting the
2798	sections-over-\(U\) functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\), and we set
2799	\(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2800	\mathcal{F})\). The corepresenting object is built \emph{without} the extension-by-zero
2801	functor — it is the sheafification of the free module on the representable presheaf
2802	\(\mathbf{y}\,U\) — and the realization keeps the injective module in the \emph{second}
2803	Ext argument, where Mathlib supplies all three facts off the shelf with \emph{no} appeal
2804	to restriction of module sheaves. The corepresenting object and its corepresentability
2805	are fixed first, then the following definition fixes the realization, and the Mathlib
2806	dependency anchors after it pin the exact results it rests on.
2807	
2808	\begin{definition}
2809	
2810	\leanok
2811	[Extension-by-zero structure sheaf as a corepresenting object]
2812	  \label{def:jshriek_ou}
2813	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU}
2814	  \uses{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction,
2815	    lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2816	  \textit{Corepresenting object for sections over an open, built without the
2817	  extension-by-zero functor.}
2818	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subscheme, regarded through the
2819	  representable presheaf \(\mathbf{y}\,U\) on the site of opens of \(X\). The
2820	  \emph{extension-by-zero structure sheaf} \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) is the object of
2821	  \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) obtained by sheafifying the free
2822	  presheaf of modules on \(\mathbf{y}\,U\):
2823	  \[
2824	    j_!\mathcal{O}_U \;:=\; \operatorname{sheafification}\bigl(
2825	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)\bigr)
2826	    \;\in\; X.\mathrm{Modules},
2827	  \]
2828	  where \(\operatorname{free}\) is the free presheaf-of-modules functor on the
2829	  representable (the degree-zero generator of the free presheaf complex of
2830	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_free_presheaf_complex}) and
2831	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) is the sheafification adjunction of
2832	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction}. Up to isomorphism this is the classical
2833	  extension-by-zero \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) of the structure sheaf of \(U\) along the open
2834	  immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\); crucially, we build only this \emph{object} as a
2835	  sheafified free module, never the extension-by-zero \emph{functor} \(j_!\). The object
2836	  exists unconditionally: the free presheaf of modules on a representable and the
2837	  sheafification of presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules are both supplied by Mathlib.
2838	\end{definition}
2839	
2840	\begin{lemma}
2841	
2842	\leanok
2843	[Corepresentability of global sections over \(U\)]
2844	  \label{lem:jshriek_corepr}
2845	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.jShriekOU_homEquiv}
2846	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2847	  \textit{The object \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the sections-over-\(U\) functor.}
2848	  For every \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) there is a natural additive
2849	  isomorphism
2850	  \[
2851	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\, \mathcal{F}\bigr)
2852	    \;\cong\;
2853	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F}) \;=\; \mathcal{F}(U),
2854	  \]
2855	  natural in \(\mathcal{F}\). Thus \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\) corepresents the global-sections
2856	  functor \(\mathcal{F} \mapsto \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\).
2857	\end{lemma}
2858	
2859	\begin{proof}
2860	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:mod_pmod_adjunction, lem:cech_complex_hom_identification}
2861	  \leanok
2862	  Compose two natural additive isomorphisms. The sheafification adjunction
2863	  \(\operatorname{sheafification} \dashv \iota\) of
2864	  Lemma~\ref{lem:mod_pmod_adjunction} transports corepresentability through
2865	  sheafification:
2866	  \[
2867	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}\bigl(
2868	      \operatorname{sheafification}(\operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U)),\, \mathcal{F}
2869	    \bigr)
2870	    \;\cong\;
2871	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2872	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr).
2873	  \]
2874	  The free–Yoneda additive equivalence \(\operatorname{freeYonedaHomAddEquiv}\) of
2875	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_complex_hom_identification} then identifies the right-hand
2876	  presheaf Hom-group with the evaluation \((\iota\mathcal{F})(U) = \mathcal{F}(U) =
2877	  \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\):
2878	  \[
2879	    \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{PMod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}\bigl(
2880	      \operatorname{free}(\mathbf{y}\,U),\, \iota\mathcal{F}\bigr)
2881	    \;\cong\;
2882	    \mathcal{F}(U).
2883	  \]
2884	  Both isomorphisms are additive and natural in \(\mathcal{F}\), so their composite is
2885	  the asserted natural additive isomorphism.
2886	\end{proof}
2887	
2888	\begin{definition}
2889	
2890	\leanok
2891	[Absolute sheaf cohomology as Ext of the corepresenting object]
2892	  \label{def:absolute_cohomology}
2893	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomology}
2894	  \uses{def:jshriek_ou, lem:jshriek_corepr, lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib,
2895	    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib,
2896	    lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib, lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
2897	  \textit{Realization of absolute sheaf cohomology in the module-sheaf category.}
2898	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\mathcal{F}\) an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(U \subseteq
2899	  X\) an open subscheme with open immersion \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\). Let
2900	  \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U \in X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be the corepresenting object of
2901	  Definition~\ref{def:jshriek_ou}. The \emph{absolute sheaf cohomology of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2902	  over \(U\)} is defined to be the Ext of this corepresenting object in the single
2903	  abelian category \(X.\mathrm{Modules} = \mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)\) of sheaves of
2904	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules:
2905	  \[
2906	    H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) \;:=\; \operatorname{Ext}^p_{\mathrm{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)}
2907	      \bigl(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,\; \mathcal{F}\bigr),
2908	    \qquad p \geq 0.
2909	  \]
2910	  Both Ext arguments live in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); no restriction of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2911	  along \(j\) is taken. Here \(\operatorname{Ext}\) is Mathlib's Ext bifunctor
2912	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}) on \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\); the
2913	  \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure it requires is available unconditionally by
2914	  Lemma~\ref{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}. The three structural facts the downstream
2915	  dimension-shift arguments consume are then exactly the corresponding Mathlib results:
2916	  \begin{enumerate}
2917	  \item \textbf{Degree zero is global sections.} In degree \(0\), Ext is the Hom-group,
2918	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2919	    \operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), by the
2920	    degree-zero Ext comparison (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}); composing
2921	    with the corepresentability isomorphism
2922	    \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F}) \cong
2923	    \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} gives \(H^0(U,
2924	    \mathcal{F}) \cong \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2925	  \item \textbf{Injective vanishing.} When the \emph{second} Ext argument is an
2926	    injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\), one has
2927	    \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(-, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) by
2928	    Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}. Since \(\mathcal{I}\) is exactly
2929	    the second argument of \(\operatorname{Ext}^{n+1}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{I})\),
2930	    this yields \(H^{n+1}(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) directly: the short exact sequence stays
2931	    in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\), no restriction is taken, and
2932	    restriction-preserves-injectives is not needed.
2933	  \item \textbf{Long exact sequence.} For a short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F}
2934	    \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) and the
2935	    \emph{fixed} first argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), the covariant Ext long exact
2936	    sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) is the long exact sequence of
2937	    the groups \(H^p(U, -)\), with connecting maps given by composition with the Ext
2938	    class of the extension.
2939	  \end{enumerate}
2940	\end{definition}
2941	
2942	\begin{definition}
2943	[Ext bifunctor in an abelian category]
2944	  \label{lem:ext_bifunctor_mathlib}
2945	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext}
2946	  \mathlibok
2947	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2948	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2949	  For an abelian category \(C\) with a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure and objects \(X, Y
2950	  \in C\) and \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), Mathlib provides the Ext group
2951	  \(\operatorname{Ext}^n_C(X, Y)\), functorial (contravariantly in \(X\), covariantly in
2952	  \(Y\)) and equipped with a composition pairing.
2953	\end{definition}

2955	\begin{lemma}
2956	[Unconditional availability of \(\mathrm{HasExt}\)]
2957	  \label{lem:hasext_standard_mathlib}
2958	  \lean{CategoryTheory.HasExt.standard}
2959	  \mathlibok
2960	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2961	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2962	  Every abelian category \(C\) carries a \(\mathrm{HasExt}\) structure; in particular the

2965	\end{lemma}
2966	
2967	\begin{lemma}
2968	[Degree-zero Ext is the Hom-group]
2969	  \label{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}
2970	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.homEquiv₀,
2971	    CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.addEquiv₀}
2972	  \mathlibok
2973	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2974	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.DerivedCategory.Ext.Basic}).}
2975	  For objects \(A, Y\) of an abelian category with \(\mathrm{HasExt}\), there is a
2976	  natural (additive) isomorphism \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(A, Y) \cong (A \to Y)\) between
2977	  degree-zero Ext and the Hom-group. Applied to the first argument \(A =
2978	  j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), this identifies \(\operatorname{Ext}^0(j_!\mathcal{O}_U,
2979	  \mathcal{F})\) with
2980	  \(\operatorname{Hom}_{X.\mathrm{Modules}}(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{F})\), which the
2981	  corepresentability isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} in turn identifies
2982	  with \(\Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\).
2983	\end{lemma}

2985	\begin{lemma}
2986	[Ext vanishes on injectives in positive degree]
2987	  \label{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}
2988	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.eq_zero_of_injective}
2989	  \mathlibok
2990	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib

30	noncomputable def jShriekOU (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) : X.Modules :=
31	  (PresheafOfModules.sheafification (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).obj
32	    ((PresheafOfModules.free X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj (yoneda.obj U))
33	
34	/-- The sheafification adjunction hom-bijection as an `AddEquiv` (additivity holds because
35	the right adjoint is an additive functor and composition is bilinear). -/
36	noncomputable def sheafificationHomAddEquiv (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) (F : X.Modules) :
37	    (jShriekOU U ⟶ F) ≃+
38	      ((PresheafOfModules.free X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj (yoneda.obj U) ⟶
39	        (Scheme.Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).obj F) :=
40	  { (PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdjunction (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).homEquiv
41	      ((PresheafOfModules.free X.ringCatSheaf.obj).obj (yoneda.obj U)) F with
42	    map_add' := by
43	      intro f g
44	      haveI : (SheafOfModules.forget X.ringCatSheaf ⋙
45	          PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars (𝟙 X.ringCatSheaf.obj)).Additive := inferInstance
46	      simp only [Equiv.toFun_as_coe, Adjunction.homEquiv_unit]
47	      erw [Functor.map_add, Preadditive.comp_add]
48	      rfl }
49	
50	noncomputable def jShriekOU_homEquiv (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X) (F : X.Modules) :
51	    (jShriekOU U ⟶ F) ≃+
52	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).obj F).presheaf.obj (Opposite.op U) :=
53	  (sheafificationHomAddEquiv U F).trans (freeYonedaHomAddEquiv U _)
54	
55	/-- **Form-B absolute cohomology** `H^p(U, F) := Ext^p_{O_X}(jShriekOU U, F)`. -/
56	noncomputable def absoluteCohomology (p : ℕ) (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
57	    (F : X.Modules) : AddCommGrpCat :=
58	  AddCommGrpCat.of (Ext (jShriekOU U) F p)
59	
60	/-- **`H⁰(U, F) ≅ Γ(U, F)`** as abelian groups: degree-zero `Ext` out of the
61	corepresenting object `jShriekOU U` recovers the sections of `F` over `U`. This is the
62	additive corepresentability of global sections specialised to `p = 0`. -/
63	noncomputable def absoluteCohomologyZeroAddEquiv (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
64	    (F : X.Modules) :
65	    Ext (jShriekOU U) F 0 ≃+
66	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheafOfModules X).obj F).presheaf.obj (Opposite.op U) :=
67	  (AddEquiv.mk' Ext.homEquiv₀ (by
68	    intro a b
69	    refine Ext.homEquiv₀.symm.injective ?_
70	    simp only [Ext.homEquiv₀_symm_apply, Ext.mk₀_add, Ext.mk₀_homEquiv₀_apply])).trans
71	    (jShriekOU_homEquiv U F)

74	modules vanishes. Direct from `Ext.eq_zero_of_injective` (the injective object is the
75	*second* `Ext` argument, so no restriction of the coefficient sheaf is needed). -/
76	theorem absoluteCohomology_eq_zero_of_injective (n : ℕ) (U : TopologicalSpace.Opens X)
77	    (I : X.Modules) [Injective I] (e : Ext (jShriekOU U) I (n + 1)) : e = 0 :=
78	  Ext.eq_zero_of_injective e

84	    {S : ShortComplex X.Modules} (hS : S.ShortExact) {n₁ : ℕ}
85	    (x₁ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₁ n₁) (hx₁ : x₁.comp (Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero n₁) = 0)
86	    {n₀ : ℕ} (hn₀ : n₀ + 1 = n₁) :
87	    ∃ x₃ : Ext (jShriekOU U) S.X₃ n₀, x₃.comp hS.extClass hn₀ = x₁ :=
88	  Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ (jShriekOU U) hS x₁ hx₁ hn₀

The {\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis
(Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}) is proved by a dimension shift on the
coefficient module, decomposed below into four sub-lemmas: the short exact sequence of
{\v C}ech complexes coming from a basis (Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}), the
resulting stability of vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology under the injective
quotient (Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}), the sheaf-cohomology base case
\(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}), and the
dimension-shift induction \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\)
(Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}). The top lemma's proof then just
chains these.

\begin{lemma}
[Short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes from a basis]
  \label{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechComplex_shortExact_of_basis}
  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:ses_cech_h1}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof, term-wise-product step) (read from
  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1776).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "By Lemma \ref{lemma-ses-cech-h1} and our assumption (2)
  % this sequence gives rise to an exact sequence
  % $$
  % 0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0.
  % $$
  % for every $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Hence for any $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
  % we get a short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
  % $$
  % 0 \to
  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
  % \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
  % $$
  % since each term in the {\v C}ech complex is made up out of a product of
  % values over elements of $\mathcal{B}$ by assumption (1)."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
  Let \(X\), \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\mathrm{Cov}\) be as in
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}, and let
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0
  \]
  be a short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules in which \(\mathcal{F}\) has
  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\) (condition (3)). Then for
  every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) the induced sequence of section {\v C}ech
  complexes
  \[
    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0
  \]
  is a short exact sequence of complexes of abelian groups.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:ses_cech_h1}
  Fix \(\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) in \(\mathrm{Cov}\). By condition (1)
  every index tuple \(i_0 \ldots i_p\) has \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\), so
  each {\v C}ech term is a product
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, -) = \prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p}
  (-)(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p})\) of section groups over basic opens. For each basic open
  \(V = U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \in \mathcal{B}\), condition (2) supplies a cofinal system
  of coverings of \(V\) lying in \(\mathrm{Cov}\), on which \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{F})\)
  vanishes by condition (3); hence Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} makes the section
  sequence
  \(0 \to \mathcal{F}(V) \to \mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V) \to 0\) exact. (The only
  nontrivial point is surjectivity of \(\mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\);
  left-exactness \(0 \to \mathcal{F}(V) \to \mathcal{I}(V) \to \mathcal{Q}(V)\) is
  automatic from left-exactness of sections on the short exact sequence of sheaves.) A
  product of short exact sequences of abelian groups is short exact, so the sequence is
  short exact in each degree \(p\). The {\v C}ech differentials are the alternating sums
  of restriction maps, which are natural in the coefficient module, so the two maps are
  chain maps; thus the displayed sequence is a short exact sequence of complexes.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Quotient preserves vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology]
  \label{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.quotient_cech_vanishing_of_basis}
  \uses{def:cech_complex, lem:cech_ses_of_basis, lem:injective_cech_acyclic}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof, long-exact-sequence step) (read from
  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1776).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "In particular we have a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech
  % cohomology groups for any open covering $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$.
  % This implies that $\mathcal{Q}$ is also an $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  % with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
  With notation as in Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}, assume moreover that
  \(\mathcal{I}\) is an injective \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the quotient
  \(\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\) again has vanishing higher {\v C}ech
  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\): \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) for all
  \(p > 0\) and every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cech_ses_of_basis, lem:injective_cech_acyclic}
  Fix \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\). The short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes
  of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} yields a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech
  cohomology
  \[
    \cdots \to \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
      \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to
      \check{H}^{p+1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to \cdots.
  \]
  For \(p > 0\) the left term \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) since
  \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}), and the right
  term \(\check{H}^{p+1}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) by condition (3) for
  \(\mathcal{F}\). Exactness forces \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\). As
  \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) was arbitrary, \(\mathcal{Q}\) has vanishing higher
  {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Sheaf-cohomology base case \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)]
  \label{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomology_one_eq_zero_of_basis}
  \uses{def:absolute_cohomology, lem:cech_ses_of_basis,
    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib,
    lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib, lem:jshriek_corepr}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof, base-case step) (read from
  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1776).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Next, we look at the long exact cohomology sequence
  % $$ \ldots $$
  % for any $U \in \mathcal{B}$. Since $\mathcal{I}$ is injective we
  % have $H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0$ for $n > 0$ (see
  % Derived Categories, Lemma \ref{derived-lemma-higher-derived-functors}).
  % By the above we see that $H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})$
  % is surjective and hence $H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
  Let \(X\), \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\mathrm{Cov}\) be as in
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}, let \(\mathcal{F}\) have vanishing higher
  {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\), and let
  \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) be a short exact sequence
  in \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) with \(\mathcal{I}\) injective. Then for every \(U \in
  \mathcal{B}\) the first absolute cohomology vanishes, \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\),
  where \(H^p(U, -)\) is the Ext realization of Definition~\ref{def:absolute_cohomology}.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{def:absolute_cohomology, lem:cech_ses_of_basis,
    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib,
    lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib, lem:jshriek_corepr}
  Run the covariant Ext long exact sequence
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) of
  \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) at the fixed first
  argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), giving the exact strand
  \[
    H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \xrightarrow{\ g\ } H^0(U, \mathcal{Q})
      \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \to H^1(U, \mathcal{I}).
  \]
  By Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib} (the injective \(\mathcal{I}\) is
  the \emph{second} Ext argument) the term \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) =
  \operatorname{Ext}^1(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\). In degree \(0\) the
  identification \(H^0(U, -) = \Gamma(U, -)\) — the degree-zero Ext comparison
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}) composed with the corepresentability
  isomorphism of Lemma~\ref{lem:jshriek_corepr} — turns \(g\) into the section map
  \(\mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U)\), which is surjective by the section-level
  exactness established inside Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis} (applied at the basic
  open \(U \in \mathcal{B}\)). Surjectivity of \(g\) makes the connecting map
  \(\delta = 0\); since also \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\), the strand reads
  \(0 \to H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) \to 0\), whence \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[Dimension-shift induction \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\)]
  \label{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.absoluteCohomology_eq_zero_of_basis}
  \uses{def:absolute_cohomology, lem:quotient_vanishing_cech,
    lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing, lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib,
    lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01EO, Lemma
  % \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof, induction step) (read from
  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L1695--1776).
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Since $\mathcal{F}$ was an arbitrary $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
  % with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for all $\mathcal{U} \in \text{Cov}$
  % we conclude that also $H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0$ since $\mathcal{Q}$ is
  % another of these sheaves (see above). By the long exact sequence this in
  % turn implies that $H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0$. And so on and so forth."
  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01EO,
  \texttt{lemma-cech-vanish-basis} (proof).}
  Let \(X\), \(\mathcal{B}\), \(\mathrm{Cov}\) be as in
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}. For every \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
  \(\mathcal{F}\) with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\), every
  \(U \in \mathcal{B}\), and every \(p > 0\), the absolute cohomology vanishes,
  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech, lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing,
    lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib, lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}
  Induct on \(p \ge 1\) with the statement quantified over \emph{all}
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{F}\) having vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology
  for \(\mathrm{Cov}\) simultaneously, so that the quotient may take the role of
  \(\mathcal{F}\) at the next stage. Fix such an \(\mathcal{F}\), embed
  \(\mathcal{F} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{I}\) into an injective
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and put \(\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\). By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech} the quotient \(\mathcal{Q}\) again has
  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\), so it is itself a valid
  instance of the inductive statement.

  \emph{Base case \(p = 1\):} immediate from
  Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing} applied to \(\mathcal{F}\).

  \emph{Step \(p \Rightarrow p+1\):} the covariant Ext long exact sequence
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib}) of
  \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) at \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\)
  gives the exact strand
  \[
    H^p(U, \mathcal{Q}) \xrightarrow{\ \delta\ } H^{p+1}(U, \mathcal{F}) \to
      H^{p+1}(U, \mathcal{I}).
  \]
  The right term vanishes since \(\mathcal{I}\) is injective
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib}), and the left term
  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) by the inductive hypothesis applied to \(\mathcal{Q}\)
  (admissible by the previous paragraph). Exactness forces
  \(H^{p+1}(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). This completes the induction, giving
  \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and all \(U \in \mathcal{B}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[{\v C}ech-to-cohomology comparison on a basis]
  \label{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cech_eq_cohomology_of_basis}
  \uses{def:cech_complex, def:absolute_cohomology}

  \uses{lem:injective_cech_acyclic, lem:ses_cech_h1, lem:cech_acyclic_affine,
    def:absolute_cohomology, lem:jshriek_corepr, lem:ext_covariant_les_mathlib,
    lem:ext_eq_zero_of_injective_mathlib, lem:ext_homequiv_zero_mathlib}
  Throughout, \(H^p(U, -)\) denotes the Ext realization of absolute sheaf cohomology
  fixed in Definition~\ref{def:absolute_cohomology}. Say that an
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module has \emph{vanishing higher {\v C}ech
  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)} if condition (3) holds for it. We argue by a
  dimension shift, taking \(\mathcal{F}\) (with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) as the inductive variable and using no input from
  affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing.

  Embed \(\mathcal{F} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{I}\) into an injective
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{I}\). By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic} the injective \(\mathcal{I}\) has
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every cover, in
  particular for every \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\). Put \(\mathcal{Q} =
  \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\), giving the short exact sequence
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0.
  \]
  For each \(U \in \mathcal{B}\) the coverings of \(\mathrm{Cov}\) are cofinal among
  coverings of \(U\) (condition (2)) and \(\check{H}^1(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
  0\) for them, so Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} makes the sequence exact on sections,
  \[
    0 \to \mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{I}(U) \to \mathcal{Q}(U) \to 0,
    \qquad U \in \mathcal{B}.
  \]
  Since by condition (1) every term of a {\v C}ech complex for a cover
  \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\) is a product of values over elements of
  \(\mathcal{B}\), this section-level exactness yields a short exact sequence of
  {\v C}ech complexes
  \[
    0 \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \to
      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) \to
      \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{Q}) \to 0,
  \]
  and hence a long exact sequence of {\v C}ech cohomology. As
  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) =
  0\) for \(p > 0\), the long exact sequence forces \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{Q}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\): the quotient \(\mathcal{Q}\) again has
  vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\).

  \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) over a fixed \(U \in \mathcal{B}\). The short

  Definition~\ref{def:absolute_cohomology} the groups \(H^n(U, -) =
  \operatorname{Ext}^n(j_!\mathcal{O}_U, -)\) are covariant in the second variable with

  0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every \(U \in \mathcal{B}\).

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:cech_ses_of_basis, lem:quotient_vanishing_cech,
    lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing, lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing,
    def:absolute_cohomology}
  Throughout, \(H^p(U, -)\) denotes the Ext realization of absolute sheaf cohomology
  fixed in Definition~\ref{def:absolute_cohomology}, and an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is
  said to have \emph{vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)} when
  condition (3) holds for it. The proof is a dimension shift on \(\mathcal{F}\), assembled
  from the four sub-lemmas above; it uses no input from affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing.

  Embed \(\mathcal{F} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{I}\) into an injective
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and put \(\mathcal{Q} = \mathcal{I}/\mathcal{F}\), giving the
  short exact sequence \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) in
  \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\). The section-level exactness furnished by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:ses_cech_h1} (under conditions (2)--(3)) together with the
  term-wise-product shape of {\v C}ech complexes (condition (1)) gives, for each
  \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathrm{Cov}\), the short exact sequence of {\v C}ech complexes of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_ses_of_basis}. Its long exact sequence, with
  \(\check{H}^{>0}(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{I}) = 0\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_cech_acyclic}) and \(\check{H}^{>0}(\mathcal{U},
  \mathcal{F}) = 0\) (condition (3)), shows that \(\mathcal{Q}\) again has vanishing
  higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:quotient_vanishing_cech}).

  Now run the sheaf-cohomology long exact sequence over a fixed \(U \in \mathcal{B}\).
  The covariant Ext long exact sequence of \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{I} \to
  \mathcal{Q} \to 0\) at the fixed first argument \(j_!\mathcal{O}_U\), with
  \(H^0(U, -) = \Gamma(U, -)\) and the section-level surjectivity \(\mathcal{I}(U) \to
  \mathcal{Q}(U)\), yields the base case \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_one_vanishing}). Feeding \(\mathcal{Q}\) back as
  \(\mathcal{F}\) and iterating the dimension shift — legitimate because \(\mathcal{Q}\)
  is again a module with vanishing higher {\v C}ech cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\) —
  gives \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every \(U \in \mathcal{B}\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}), which is the assertion.

  theorem cechComplex_shortExact_of_basis {X : Scheme.{u}} {U : Opens X}
      (𝒰 : <open cover of U, e.g. the project's cover datum with face opens U_{i₀…i_p}>)
      {S : ShortComplex X.Modules} (hS : S.ShortExact)
      (hsec : ∀ (i : <index tuple of 𝒰 in any degree>),

      : (sectionCechComplexShortComplex 𝒰 hS).ShortExact   -- HomologicalComplex.ShortExact of section Čech complexes

  theorem quotient_cech_vanishing_of_basis {X : Scheme.{u}} {U : Opens X}
      (𝒰 : <cover of U>) {S : ShortComplex X.Modules} (hS : S.ShortExact)
      (hSES : (sectionCechComplexShortComplex 𝒰 hS).ShortExact)    -- from L1
      (hI : ∀ p, 0 < p → IsZero (cechCohomology 𝒰 S.X₂ p))         -- injective_cech_acyclic

  ```

  theorem absoluteCohomology_one_eq_zero_of_basis {X : Scheme.{u}} (U : Opens X)

  -- bespoke, lightweight: a cover system on a basis (conditions (1)+(2) of 01EO)
  structure BasisCovSystem (X : Scheme.{u}) where
    B    : Set (Opens X)                              -- basis, closed under the relevant ∩
    Cov  : Set (Σ U : Opens X, <cover of U>)          -- the admissible covers
    faces_mem : ∀ c ∈ Cov, U c ∈ B ∧ ∀ tuple, faceOpen c tuple ∈ B          -- (1)
    cofinal  : ∀ V ∈ B, <coverings of V in Cov are cofinal among all coverings of V>  -- (2)
  -- per-F part of conditions (3): the inductive predicate (abstract module, NOT QCoh)
  def HasVanishingHigherCech (s : BasisCovSystem X) (F : X.Modules) : Prop :=

  theorem absoluteCohomology_eq_zero_of_basis (s : BasisCovSystem X)
      {F : X.Modules} (hF : HasVanishingHigherCech s F)

      : ∀ e : Ext (jShriekOU U) F p, e = 0
